content: The OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) model splits network communication into 7 layers, each with a distinct responsibility, from bottom to top: Physical (raw bits over a medium — cables, radio), Data Link (framing, MAC addresses — e.g. Ethernet), Network (routing, IP addresses), Transport (reliable delivery — TCP, or fast/unreliable — UDP), Session (managing connections), Presentation (data format/encryption), Application (the protocols apps actually use — HTTP, FTP, DNS). It's a conceptual model for reasoning about networking, not literally how every real system is implemented — the TCP/IP model (4 layers) is the more commonly implemented reference in practice.
example:
A web request roughly maps like this:
Application: HTTP request
Transport: TCP breaks it into segments, adds port numbers
Network: IP adds source/destination addresses, routes the packet
Data Link: Ethernet frames it for the local network
Physical: sent as electrical/optical/radio signals

quiz.question: Which OSI layer is responsible for routing based on IP addresses?
Name two protocols that operate at the Transport layer.

references: Computer Networking: A Top-Down Approach, Kurose & Ross, Ch. 1

content: An IP address uniquely identifies a device on a network. IPv4 addresses are 32 bits, written as four decimal numbers 0-255 separated by dots (e.g. 192.168.1.1) — giving about 4.3 billion possible addresses, which is why IPv6 (128-bit addresses) was introduced for the long term. A subnet mask (e.g. 255.255.255.0, often written /24) determines which portion of an address identifies the network versus the specific host. Private IP ranges (like 192.168.0.0/16 and 10.0.0.0/8) are reserved for local networks and aren't routable on the public internet directly — NAT (Network Address Translation) lets many private devices share one public IP.
example:
IP: 192.168.1.10
Subnet mask: 255.255.255.0 (/24)
→ Network portion: 192.168.1.0
→ Host portion: .10
→ Usable host range on this subnet: 192.168.1.1 to 192.168.1.254

quiz.question: How many bits does an IPv4 address use?
192.168.1.5 is a publicly routable IP address.

references: Computer Networking: A Top-Down Approach, Kurose & Ross, Ch. 4

content: HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol) is how browsers and servers exchange web content — requests (GET, POST, etc.) and responses (status codes, headers, body). HTTPS is HTTP layered over TLS (Transport Layer Security) encryption: the same protocol, but the connection is encrypted end-to-end and the server's identity is verified via a certificate. Without HTTPS, data (including passwords) travels in plain text and can be intercepted on the network. Browsers now flag plain HTTP sites as 'Not Secure' for this reason.
example:
http://example.com   → unencrypted, vulnerable to interception
https://example.com  → encrypted via TLS, server identity verified by certificate

A typical HTTP request:
GET /index.html HTTP/1.1
Host: example.com

quiz.question: What does HTTPS add on top of HTTP?
Data sent over plain HTTP can be read by anyone intercepting the network traffic.
